ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002572 covers hamburger broadly; double-patty variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM covers hamburger; double-patty configuration is not a distinct FoodEx2 node. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for double beef burger identified in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One double beef burger weighing approximately 220 g, comprising two grilled beef patties, a toasted bun, cheese, lettuce, tomato, and onion.

nutrient_source.title: Double Beef Burger — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Double Beef Burger (cr_id F05020037). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, and micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry are set to -1 where unavailable; values estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, USDA #21108 Beef patty, cooked, and USDA #18350 Hamburger bun) are used for saturated fat and cholesterol as supplementary estimates and are documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Hamburger bun / white bread composite analogue
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for double beef burger exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 66 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle: white hamburger bun (GI ~66, University of Sydney GI Database, white bread/bun category). Beef patties and cheese contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (66 × 14.56 g/100g × 220 g serving) / 100 = 21.2 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 505 mg per 100 g — well above the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern, driven by processed cheese, condiments, and seasoned beef patties.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit or avoid double beef burgers; if consumed, request no added condiments or pickles and choose a smaller portion to keep sodium intake within daily targets (≤2000 mg/day).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White hamburger bun contributes high-GI refined carbohydrates (GI ~66); total carbohydrate 14.56 g/100 g with only 1.4 g dietary fibre produces a high glycaemic load of ~21 g per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption of double beef burgers; substituting a wholegrain bun or lettuce wrap, avoiding sugary condiments, and reducing portion size can meaningfully lower the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 15.3 g/100 g including saturated fat ~6.2 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.3 g/100 g; sodium 505 mg/100 g; cholesterol ~62 mg/100 g — all elevate cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat double beef burgers as an occasional food only; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, and high sodium significantly raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk with frequent consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Double beef patties are red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways; saturated fat ~6.2 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.3 g/100 g both exceed RA thresholds and are pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption; the arachidonic acid, saturated fat, and trans fat content of this burger can exacerbate systemic inflammation and joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A double beef burger consists of two grilled ground-beef patties stacked between a toasted white hamburger bun, typically accompanied by a slice of processed cheddar cheese, fresh lettuce leaves, sliced tomato, and raw or pickled onion. The patties are seasoned and cooked on a grill or flat-top, yielding a browned, slightly charred exterior with a juicy interior. The overall flavour profile is savoury and rich, with the cheese adding creaminess, the vegetables providing freshness and crunch, and the toasted bun contributing a mild sweetness. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion delivers 251 kcal, 13.75 g protein, 15.3 g fat (including approximately 6.2 g saturated fat and 0.3 g trans fat), and 14.56 g carbohydrate with 1.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 505 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is approximately 66 (medium), driven by the white bun, producing a glycaemic load of about 21 g per standard 220 g serving. Clinically, this food is a high-concern item for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI, low fibre), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat), and rheumatoid arthritis (arachidonic acid, trans fat). It is classified as halal and high-protein. The double beef burger is a staple of Western fast-food culture, widely available in Singapore and globally.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (cr_id F05020037) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (beef patty and hamburger bun composites) as the closest approved secondary source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white bun analogue with documented basis. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from primary HPB source): calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for F05020037 and are not available in sufficient detail from USDA SR Legacy for this composite dish without introducing unacceptable estimation error. Saturated fat (6.2 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (5.8 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.1 g/100g), trans fat (0.3 g/100g), potassium (220 mg/100g), and cholesterol (62 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked ground beef patty (USDA #21108) and enriched hamburger bun (USDA #18350) weighted by approximate composition (two patties ~55%, bun ~30%, cheese ~8%, vegetables ~7%); these are supplementary estimates and are flagged accordingly. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on white hamburger bun GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for double beef burger exists in the approved GI database. Glycaemic load per serving = (66 × 14.56 × 220) / 10000 = 21.2 g. This is a Western food item; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean-origin dish and no established local-language canonical names exist for this generic Western food category. The food_group 'Grains and staples' and food_subgroup 'Bread-based dishes' are preserved from the HPB source classification.
